title-group.article-title: Trajectories of Physical Disabilities Over 6 Months in Patients With Long COVID

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author: Jean-Sébastien Roy, PT, Phd, Center for Interdisciplinary Research in Rehabilitation and Social Integration (Cirris), CIUSSS Capitale-Nationale, Quebec City, QC, Canada (imane.salmam.1@ulaval.ca)

All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

sec.title: Importance
sec.p: Understanding the long-term impact of long COVID on physical function and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) is essential to guide clinical care and rehabilitation.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The objective of this study was to compare physical capacity over time among adults in 3 groups: those without COVID-19 (control group [CG]), those who recovered from COVID without persistent symptoms (short COVID group [SCG]), and those with long COVID (long COVID group [LCG]). A secondary objective was to identify baseline predictors of 6-month HRQoL in the LCG.

sec.title: Design
sec.p: This study was a prospective longitudinal cohort study.

sec.title: Setting
sec.p: In-laboratory assessments were conducted at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months, at either the Center for Interdisciplinary Research in Rehabilitation and Social Integration (Quebec City) or the Orthopedic Clinical Research Unit (Montreal).

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: A total of 360 age- and sex-matched adults (n = 120 per group), including individuals without a history of COVID-19 (CG), those with short COVID (symptom resolution within 4 weeks, SCG), and those with persistent symptoms ≥12 weeks (LCG) participated in the study.

sec.title: Intervention/Exposure
sec.p: Participants were categorized based on their COVID-19 history and symptom duration and no intervention or exposure was applied.

sec.title: Main Outcomes and Measures
sec.p: Self-reported outcomes measuring HRQoL, comorbidities, sleep quality, pain, and fatigue, along with objective measures such as grip strength, Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB), 6-min walk test (6MWT), and perceived exertion (Modified Borg Scale) during the 6MWT, were collected. Daily averages for resting heart rate, step count, and minutes of intensive activity were recorded over 7 days using a fitness tracker watch. Generalized estimating equations were used for longitudinal comparisons, and recursive partitioning analysis for predicting HRQoL factors.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Significant time × group interactions were observed for HRQoL, sleep quality, pain, fatigue, SPPB, and 6MWT. Although the LCG showed significant improvements across these outcomes, only the reduction in fatigue reached a clinically meaningful level, whereas the other groups remained stable. A group effect was detected for all outcomes, except for heart rate and minutes of intensive activity, with the LCG consistently showing lower scores across all follow-ups. Recursive partitioning analysis identified 2 baseline predictors of HRQoL at 6 months in the LCG: self-reported fatigue and daily step count.

sec.title: Conclusions and Relevance
sec.p: These findings highlight the persistent impairments in adults with long COVID and emphasize early HRQoL predictor identification to anticipate long-term needs and adjust treatment plans accordingly.

institution-wrap.institution: Réseau Provincial de Recherche en Adaptation-Réadaptation (REPAR)

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Contracting COVID-19 poses a risk of developing long COVID, a condition where individuals experience persistent symptoms and long-term health issues even after recovering from the initial infection. Previous findings have demonstrated that long COVID significantly affects individuals’ cognitive and physical functions, resulting in decreased health-related quality of life (HRQoL),1 worsened sleep quality,2 and reduced physical capacities.3

An online cohort study of 968 adults with confirmed COVID-19 infection tracked symptoms, revealing that 85% of participants still experienced some symptoms 1 year after their infection.4 Recent studies support these findings, indicating that factors such as comorbidities and obesity may increase the likelihood of developing long COVID.5,6 These factors are believed to contribute to immune system dysregulation, characterized by heightened inflammation and oxidative stress, which may prolong and exacerbate COVID-19 symptoms.7

Although knowledge of the physical limitations associated with long COVID has increased, there remains a gap regarding the trajectory of physical health over time. Specifically, there is a lack of comprehensive data on the long-term progression of physical capacities, the variability in recovery, and the underlying mechanisms driving these changes. This gap hinders the identification of factors influencing recovery or HRQoL, as well as the development of effective strategies to enhance HRQoL. For instance, a prospective study of 299 patients hospitalized for COVID-19 assessed their condition at 3 and 12 months. At 3 months, 20% of patients experienced a decline in their 6-min walking distance, which decreased to 7% by 12 months.8 This suggests that 13% of patients experienced recovery; however, a subset continued to face persistent impairments at 12 months. Additionally, another study evaluating 135 individuals with long COVID between 1 and 3 months after their diagnosis reported loss of muscle mass and a decreased distance in the 6-min walk test (6MWT).9 Although these impairments may be influenced by factors beyond long COVID, they highlight the need for further investigation into long-term recovery trajectories.

However, results from existing studies are mostly limited by samples of hospitalized individuals, online survey data, lack of control groups (CGs), or limited longitudinal follow-up of objective performance measures and self-reported outcomes. Given the profound effects of fatigue, pain, and reduced physical capacities on HRQoL, comprehensive tracking of this condition is essential for developing effective interventions and assisting health care providers in addressing the evolving needs of adults with long COVID.

The primary objective of this study was to compare the longitudinal evolution of physical capacities and level of functioning between adults who did not develop COVID-19, adults who developed COVID-19 but did not experience persistent symptoms, and adults with long COVID. The secondary objective was to explore potential risk factors including sociodemographic, self-reported, and clinical variables in adults with long COVID that may predict HRQoL 6 months later. Unlike previous studies with cross-sectional data or brief follow-ups, our longitudinal design provides deeper insights into symptom progression and recovery, aiding in the development of targeted rehabilitation strategies. Additionally, identifying key determinants and risk factors for individuals with long COVID that may predict long-term HRQoL will enable health care professionals to anticipate future needs and adjust treatment plans accordingly.

We hypothesized that individuals with long COVID would exhibit lower physical capacities compared to the other 2 groups, with minimal improvement during the 6-month follow-up period. Second, we hypothesized that baseline variables such as advanced age, preexisting comorbidities, and higher fatigue level would be significant predictors of lower HRQoL at 6 months.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: This longitudinal population-based cohort study comprised 3 evaluation sessions: baseline, 3 months, and 6 months. The baseline evaluation was conducted at least 3 months after infection for the COVID groups.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Three cohorts of adults, matched for age and sex, were enrolled. The CG comprised adults who, on the basis of self-report, did not develop COVID-19 (no antibody or serology test was conducted to confirm prior exposure). The short COVID group (SCG) consisted of adults with confirmed prior COVID-19 infection (polymerase chain reaction or rapid antigen test) but no persistent symptoms beyond 4 weeks. The long COVID group (LCG) consisted of adults (>18 years old) with confirmed prior COVID-19 infection (polymerase chain reaction or rapid antigen test) who reported persistent symptoms lasting at least 12 weeks, aligning with the World Health Organization definition, which describes long COVID as the continuation or development of new symptoms 3 months after the initial SARS-CoV-2 infection, with these symptoms lasting for at least 2 months and having no other explanation.10,11 The recruitment strategy involved various sources, including medical clinics in Quebec City and Montreal, electronic mailing lists of students, active and retired employees at Université Laval, and social media platforms such as Facebook.

Recruitment for the study took place between June 2021 and March 2023. The last assessment and end of follow-up was conducted in September 2023. The project received ethical approval from both the Comité d'Éthique de la Recherche du CIUSSS de la Capitale-Nationale (2022-2328) and the Comité d'Éthique de la Recherche du CIUSSS de l'Est-de-l'Île-de-Montréal (2022-2811). The study protocol was registered on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05216536) and published,12 providing additional insights into the study’s methodology, the selection of variables, and their respective psychometric properties. This article focuses on physical impairments, defined as limitations in physical capacity and mobility.

sec.title: Experimental Procedures
sec.p: Eligible participants were sent an email with a link to an online informed consent form. All procedures were thoroughly explained in the online informed consent form and during a screening phone call to ensure that participants could safely complete the evaluations while respecting their individual limitations. Baseline web-based self-administered questionnaires (hosted on the Research Electronic Data Capture [REDCap] platform)13 were then emailed to participants to evaluate HRQoL, comorbidity, sleep quality, pain and pain-related disabilities, and fatigue. Participants had the option to complete the web-based questionnaires over a period of 5 days, especially for the LCG if they experienced fatigue. Alternatively, those without internet access were able to complete the questionnaires during the in-laboratory evaluation. Participants were assigned unique group codes in REDCap: PC (participant with long COVID), PX (participant with short COVID), and PS (participant without COVID history), followed by a numerical identifier (eg, PC-001, PX-002). The LCG also completed a questionnaire on COVID-19-related symptoms, infection history, and symptom duration.

Within 1 week of enrollment, participants were scheduled for an in-laboratory evaluation, conducted either in Quebec City or Montreal. During this session, participants provided sociodemographic information such as age, sex, educational level, employment status, and annual income, as well as their vaccination status, and had their weight and height measured. Those in the COVID groups provided comprehensive information about their COVID-19 history and symptoms. For the LCG, symptoms were evaluated using a custom-designed open-ended questionnaire, enabling participants to report additional symptoms beyond those listed. This questionnaire was developed on the basis of the most commonly reported symptoms associated with long COVID.14 Following this, participants underwent objective clinical tests to evaluate their physical capacity, including assessments of grip strength, balance, gait speed, and gait endurance. Lastly, participants were instructed to wear a fitness tracker watch for 7 days. The same data collection procedures were repeated at 3 and 6 months. Rest periods were provided as necessary to support the needs of participants with long COVID.

Consistency was maintained throughout the evaluation process, with all participants undergoing tests in a uniform sequence. The sequence of physical tests was selected from the least to the most strenuous. Breaks were provided as necessary, and evaluators, having received comprehensive training, adhered to standardized procedures. Regular standardization meetings were conducted among evaluators, collectively ensuring a consistent, reliable, and unbiased testing process.

sec.title: Self-Reported Questionnaires
sec.p: HRQoL was assessed using the EuroQol Group 5-Dimension 5-Level Questionnaire (EQ-5D-5L), which measures 5 dimensions (mobility, self-care, usual activities, pain/discomfort, and anxiety/depression) on a 5-point scale.15 The total score on the EQ-5D-5L is transformed to range between 0 and 1, with 1 indicating excellent HRQoL and 0 representing the poorest HRQoL.16

Comorbidity level was evaluated using the Self-Administered Comorbidity Questionnaire, which captures information on 12 common medical conditions, including treatment and resulting activity limitations. Each affirmative response earned 1 point, allowing for a maximum of 3 points for each condition. Given the 12 predefined medical issues and 3 additional open-field medical condition questions, participants could attain a maximum global score of 45 points.17

Sleep quality was measured with the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI), which assesses the quality and patterns of sleep over the past month.18 A 4-point Likert scale (ranging from 0–3) is used to assess 7 distinct aspects of sleep, with scores ranging from 0 to 21. A higher score indicates more severe sleep disturbances.19

Pain and pain-related disabilities were quantified using the Brief Pain Inventory Short Form (BPI), which measures pain intensity (4 items) and interference (7 items) with daily functioning through 11 items, with higher scores indicating more severe pain.20,21

Fatigue severity and its impact on daily activities were evaluated using the Fatigue Severity Scale (FSS), consisting of 9 items rated on a 7-point scale, with higher scores indicating more pronounced fatigue and greater impact on daily activities.22,23 However, because of an error during data collection, the final score was calculated on the basis of the sum of 8 items instead of 9, as the last item was omitted.

sec.title: Objective Performance Measures
sec.p: Handgrip strength assessment involved participants seated with their elbows flexed at 90 degrees and wrists in a neutral position. Using a handgrip dynamometer (Jamar; JA Preston Corp, New York, NY, USA), participants performed 3 trials for each hand, and the highest recorded strength (in kg) was selected for subsequent analysis.24–26

The Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB) was used to evaluate lower extremity function through 3 tasks mimicking daily activities: maintaining static standing balance, walking (speed), and transferring from a seated position to a standing position. Each task received a rating on a scale from 0 (inability) to 4 (highest ability level). The final score ranged from 0 to 12.27

The 6MWT was used to assess endurance and functional capacity by measuring the maximum distance participants covered within a 6-min time frame. This assessment is conducted within a corridor, typically spanning 15 to 20 m in length. Because of space constraints, participants in Montreal were tested in a 15-m corridor, whereas those in Quebec City had access to a 20-m corridor. Distance covered during the test was measured using a measuring wheel.28
Perceived exertion levels were assessed using the Modified Borg Scale (ranging from 0 for no exertion to 10 for maximal exertion) before and at each minute during the 6MWT.29

sec.title: Physical Activity
sec.p: Participants were instructed to wear a fitness tracker watch (Garmin Forerunner 35; Garmin International, Inc, Olathe, KS, USA) for 7 consecutive days (24 h/day) after the in-laboratory evaluation. The watches were provided to the participants by the research team, and all data were retrieved via the Garmin Connect platform. Daily averages for resting heart rate, number of steps, and number of minutes of intensive activity were calculated over this 7-day recording period. The Garmin Forerunner 35 has demonstrated excellent test-retest reliability and high concurrent validity for step counting.30 Additionally, heart rate validity has been reported to range from low to excellent.31 A daily step count of 5000 to 7499 steps per day reflects a “low active” lifestyle,32 and a normal resting heart rate for adults ranges between 60 and 100 beats per minute.33 In line with World Health Organization guidelines, the weekly recommendation for vigorous-intensity physical activity is 75 to 150 min,34 which corresponds to an average of 15 to 22 min of intensive activity per day.

sec.title: Sample Size
sec.p: On the basis of planned statistical analyses using generalized estimating equations (GEE) and analysis of variance with maximum-likelihood estimations, the ideal sample size per group was determined to be approximately 60 participants.35 The sample size was conservatively adjusted to 120 participants per group to account for potential attrition across the 3 evaluation time points, specifically within the CG because of the risk of COVID-19 infection during the follow-up period.

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: To compare the longitudinal variations in physical state and functioning across the 3 groups, repeated-measures analysis of variance was employed (3 groups × 3 times). The GEE approach was chosen for its robustness in handling unstructured matrices between repeated measures, its ability to provide population-averaged estimates, and its capacity to account for the correlation between repeated observations within the same participants. The best GEE model, according to the goodness-of-fit criterion (corrected quasi likelihood under the independence model criterion, QICC) was a γ distribution with a log link, except when the distribution was 0 inflated. In such cases, a Tweedie distribution (variance parameter = 1.5) was used. Each dependent variable was analyzed separately since there is no multivariate option for GEE. Post hoc analyses were performed using the east significant difference test to account for multiple comparisons. Although GEE inherently accommodate missing data, multiple imputation was also performed as a complementary analysis using the classification and regression tree method implemented in package mice in R (R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria).

To investigate the baseline predictors of HRQoL at the 6-month follow-up among adults with long COVID, decision tree analysis using recursive partitioning was conducted.36 Potential variables from the baseline assessment included sex, age, smoking status, body mass index, scores from self-reported questionnaires (FSS, Self-Administered Comorbidity Questionnaire, BPI pain, BPI interference, and PSQI), physical assessments (handgrip strength, SPPB, 6MWT, and higher perception of effort), and Garmin data (resting heart rate, maximum heart rate, minutes of intensive activity, and step count). The decision tree analysis was conducted with the parent node set to 40 and the child node set to 15, employing χ-square automatic interaction detection exhaustive analysis.
All statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS statistics (IBM statistics 29.0.0.0; IBM SPSS, Chicago, IL, USA) except for the multiple imputation, which was conducted in R (version 4.5.0).

sec.title: Role of the Funding Source
sec.p: The funders played no role in the design, conduct, or reporting of this study.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: A total of 360 adults participated in this study, distributed across 3 groups of 120 adults. The mean ages at baseline were 46.0 (SD = 15.9), 42.1 (SD = 16.4), and 44.2 (SD = 11.2) years for CG, SCG, and LCG, respectively (P > .05). The LCG consisted of 102 women, whereas the CG and SCG each contained 90 women. The mean body mass indexes at baseline were 27.4 (SD = 6.8), 25.4 (SD = 5.1), and 27.5 (SD = 6.2) for CG, SCG, and LCG, respectively. The mean times since infection at baseline were 274 (SD = 218) and 329 (SD = 146) days for SCG and LCG, respectively. Over the study period, the percentage of participants in the LCG not receiving any treatment increased from 11.7% at baseline to 13.5% at 6 months. Consultations with general practitioners remained high but decreased from 56.7% to 40.4%. Meanwhile, the use of COVID-19-related medications, prescribed during or after infection and still used during the study period, dropped from 57.5% to 30.3%. Participation in rehabilitation interventions provided by physical and occupational therapists increased from 15% at baseline to 23.6% at 6 months.

At the 3-month follow-up, participation rates were 75.0% for CG, 91.7% for SCG, and 82.5% for LCG. At the 6-month follow-up, participation rates were 53.3% for CG, 88.3% for SCG, and 76.7% for LCG. The lower participation rate (53.3%) in the CG at 6 months was mainly attributed to the loss of participants who contracted COVID-19 during the study period (Figure 1). The symptoms that were prominent throughout the study period were fatigue (≥75%), difficulty concentrating (≥67%), shortness of breath (≥61%), memory impairment (≥61%), sleep disorders (≥51%), and joint and muscle pain (≥51%) (Table 1).

caption.p: Flow of Participants Through the Study.
Symptoms Experienced by Participants With Long COVID

th.bold: No. (%) of Participants Who Still Experienced Symptoms at:

sec.p: For self-reported questionnaires, significant group × time interactions were observed for HRQoL (EQ-5D-5L [P = .007]), sleep quality (PSQI [P = .01]), pain interference (BPI interference [P < .001]), and fatigue (FSS [P = .05]), with post hoc tests indicating statistically significant improvements in the LCG, whereas the other groups remained stable during the study period. However, no clinically significant changes were observed for HRQoL, pain interference, and sleep quality in the LCG, as the changes did not exceed the minimal clinically important difference (MCID) for the EQ-5D-5L (MCID = 0.26 points),37 BPI interference (MCID = 2.3 points),38 and PSQI (MCID = 1.5–3.0 points).39,40 For fatigue, the improvements were clinically significant. The change in the FSS score from baseline to 6 months (4 points) fell within the MCID range for the FSS (6.4%–12.6%).41

Furthermore, a statistically significant group effect was observed for all questionnaires (P < .001), indicating that participants in the LCG had higher levels of fatigue, pain, and comorbidities and lower sleep quality and HRQoL throughout the study than participants in the CG and SCG. GEE outputs are reported in Table 2, and individual data are shown in Figure 2 via violin plots, with median variations shown as differences between horizontal lines.

fn.label: a
fn.p: Abbreviations: 6MWT = 6-min walk test; BPI = Brief Pain Inventory; CG = control group; EQ-5D-5L = EuroQol Group 5-Dimension 5-Level Questionnaire; FSS = Fatigue Severity Scale; HGS = handgrip strength; HR = heart rate; LCG = long COVID group; PSQI = Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index; SCG = short COVID group; SCQ = Self-Administered Comorbidity Questionnaire; SPPB = Short Physical Performance Battery.

fn.label: c
fn.p: Bold type indicates a significant difference (P < .05).

fn.label: d
fn.p: Data regarding physical activity were gathered from all participants. Any missing data were due to malfunctions with the watches.

caption.p: Self-Reported Measures Across Assessments. Health-related quality of life was measured with the euroQol group 5-dimension 5-level questionnaire. Abbreviations: BPI = Brief Pain Inventory; FSS = Fatigue Severity Scale; PSQI = Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index; SCQ = Self-Administered Comorbidity Questionnaire.

sec.p: For physical assessments, statistically significant group × time interactions were identified for SPPB (P < .001) and 6MWT (P = .03), with post hoc tests indicating statistically significant improvements in the LCG, whereas the other groups remained stable during the study period. Post hoc tests indicate statistically significant improvements in the LCG between baseline and the 3-month assessment and between baseline and the 6-month assessment in 6MWT (P < .006) and SPPB (P < .001). The MCID for the SPPB is generally around 1 point.42,43 The observed increase in the SPPB in the LCG from baseline to 6 months was 0.6 point, which falls short of the accepted MCID. For the 6MWT, a change of 45 m is considered clinically significant.44 The mean change observed in the LCG was 27.5 m, suggesting that this improvement was not clinically significant.

Furthermore, significant group effects were observed (P < .05), with the LCG showing lower performance in HGS (P = .004), SPPB (P < .001), 6MWT (P < .001), and step count (P < .001) as well as a higher perception of effort (P < .001) and mean heart rate (P < .001) than the CG and SCG (Figure 2, Table 2).

All outcomes were also analyzed using multiple imputation, and results for the group × time interaction remained consistent with the complete-case analysis, except for BPI pain severity (P < .001) and the 6MWT (P = .3). These findings suggest that, contrary to the initial analysis, physical performance as measured by the 6MWT did not improve significantly over time. Conversely, pain severity, which was previously not significant, appeared to decrease meaningfully. Full results from the imputed models are available in the Table S1.

The recursive partitioning analysis for HRQoL at 6 months in the LCG identified 2 significant baseline predictors: the FSS score (self-reported fatigue) and the step count (average number of steps taken daily over a 7-day period, as recorded with the Garmin device) (Figure 3). The risk estimate was 0.01, with an SE of 0.002, indicating high precision and robustness of the model. The distributions of cases and means in nodes 1, 2, and 3 were 29.2%, 25.8%, and 44.9%, respectively, showing significant differences in the HRQoL at 6 months, with means of 0.95, 0.84, and 0.70, respectively. The first node splits into 2 on the basis of the FSS score, making it the most important variable for predicting HRQoL at 6 months. In addition to the total FSS score, each pruned regression tree retained step count as a secondary variable, allowing further classification of participants into subgroups. The cutoff for the total FSS score at the first node was 45.0. Participants with an FSS score of ≤45.0 had the highest predicted HRQoL (mean = 0.95; n = 26). Conversely, participants with an FSS score of >45.0 and a step count of ≤5570 had a lower predicted HRQoL (mean = 0.70; n = 40), whereas those with a step count of >5570 had a higher predicted HRQoL (mean = 0.84; n = 23).

caption.p: Results of the Recursive Partitioning Analysis. Abbreviations: df1 = degrees of freedom numerator; df2 = degrees of freedom denominator; F = F-statistic; SD = standard deviation.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Our study reveals that individuals experiencing long COVID exhibited consistently lower physical capacities over 6 months as measured by the 6MWT and SPPB, with only minimal improvements that did not reach clinical significance. However, for LCG, self-reported scores related to fatigue, HRQoL, sleep quality, and pain demonstrated small, gradual improvement over 6-month assessment, with potential clinically significant progress in the FSS scores. Despite these improvements, their physical capacities remained lower and their levels of pain and fatigue were higher than those of the other 2 groups. Furthermore, our study showed that lower fatigue levels and higher physical activity (measured by average daily step count) at baseline are significant predictors of better HRQoL 6 months later.

Despite some improvement, individuals in the LCG remained at a reduced capacity. For example, the percentages on the predicted 6MWT (expected performance based on age, sex, and height, as described by Enright and Sherrill45) achieved by participants with long COVID were 77.4% (SD = 23.6), 81.9% (SD = 23.1), and 81.9% (SD = 23.0) at baseline, 3 months, and 6 months, respectively; in comparison, the respective percentages on the SCG over the same periods were 97.7% (SD = 15.9), 99.4% (SD = 16.9), and 99.6% (SD = 15.4). The persistence of this lower physical capacity may be attributed to factors such as deconditioning due to physical inactivity,46,47 as well as the biological effects of COVID-19 infection on the immune system. Although our study focuses on identifying physical impairments, exploring potential mechanisms leading to these limitations provides important context.

A previous study found phenotypic disturbances in both total CD4+ and CD8+ T lymphocytes among individuals with long COVID.48 These lymphocytes are crucial in the immune response to SARS-CoV-2 infection and supporting the body’s recovery.49 Disruptions in the phenotype or function of these lymphocytes have been associated with chronic inflammation and impaired tissue recovery.50 Similarly, a study employing “deformability cytometry” revealed significant differences in the blood cells of individuals with long COVID compared to those without infectious or inflammatory diseases.51 Specifically, the size and deformability of red blood cells were notably altered in individuals with long COVID, suggesting potential damage.51 Long COVID might induce lasting changes in the size and flexibility of red blood cells (erythrocytes), which could impact oxygen delivery.52 This could hinder the ability of individuals with long COVID to receive adequate oxygen in their tissues and engage in regular physical activities, partly explaining the persistent reduction in physical capacity. The multiple imputation analysis supports this interpretation by showing that 6MWT performance did not significantly improve over time when accounting for missing data, reinforcing the notion of a plateau in recovery. Although these mechanisms provide valuable context, our study did not directly assess them. Nevertheless, the findings may enhance our understanding of the persistent physical impairments observed. Furthermore, a previous study reported no significant differences in physical capacity among individuals with long COVID when stratified by time since infection.53 Instead, a higher comorbidity burden was associated with reduced functional performance. Notably, individuals with a history of hospitalization exhibited a greater comorbidity burden and elevated body mass index, which may further contribute to their reduced physical capacity.53

Our results raised an important question regarding the nature and long-term management of long COVID. Although individuals with long COVID reported subjective improvements in fatigue, sleep, and pain over time, they continued to exhibit persistently lower capacities, with only slight improvements in HRQoL. This raises concerns about a potential plateau in physical recovery and underscores the need to investigate the underlying mechanisms behind this stabilization. The observed improvements in self-reported symptoms may result from better management of exertion by individuals with long COVID. For instance, a study found that a 6-week biofeedback intervention, which taught self-regulation of autonomic function, led to improved quality of life and sleep quality.54 The improvements on some self-reported measures in our study may be linked to self-regulation efforts by individuals experiencing long COVID, such as adjusting activity intensity and modifying daily routines to accommodate symptoms. Additionally, patients with long COVID have reported employing self-care strategies, as highlighted in the study by Leggat et al,55 who found that individuals often rely on self-generated approaches such as over-the-counter medications, activity adjustments, symptom tracking, and seeking advice from social media and online resources to navigate their condition, largely because of the near absence of formal support services.55 Moreover, recent research highlights that the absence of formal support systems for individuals with long COVID poses a significant obstacle to recovery. It found that government support programs were insufficient and challenging to access across 3 key areas: occupational and financial assistance, health care services, and social and emotional support.56 Policies should therefore prioritize long-term disability support, workplace accommodations, and access to multidisciplinary care to mitigate the impact of long COVID on individuals’ quality of life.

Our results suggest that baseline fatigue score and average daily step count over 7 days (with baseline occurring, on average, 329 days after infection) are significant predictors of HRQoL at 6 months in individuals with long COVID, emphasizing the importance of addressing fatigue and promoting adapted physical activity to enhance recovery and improve HRQoL. Previous reports indicated that 18% to 60% of individuals with long COVID experience decreased HRQoL.57,58 Previous research has confirmed the association between fatigue and HRQoL in various conditions, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease,59 and other chronic conditions.60,61 The identification of step count as a second predictor, alongside fatigue levels, offers new insights into the relationship between physical activity and HRQoL. Higher step counts are linked to a better physical condition, which may correlate with milder symptoms.62,63 This highlights the importance of a balanced approach that integrates both adequate rest and appropriate physical activity. Although limited, existing systematic reviews and meta-analyses have examined the effects of exercise-based rehabilitation on long COVID.64,65 These reviews generally support the use of exercise-based rehabilitation, but emphasize the need for individualized approaches and further research on specific manifestations of long COVID. Other findings suggest that high-intensity physical exercise may provide fewer benefits than light-intensity exercise in the rehabilitation of patients with long COVID.66 These findings underscore the importance of managing long COVID through maintaining or gradually increasing exercise levels, while avoiding overexertion, to achieve significant improvements in quality of life. Exercise programs should be complemented by patient education on symptom self-management, energy conservation techniques, and adaptive pacing strategies to prevent setbacks.

sec.title: Strengths and Limitations
sec.p: Several limitations should be acknowledged when interpreting the results. The 3-month intervals between follow-up sessions may not fully capture long-term changes in variables affecting functional capacity beyond the 6-month period. Additionally, the range of variables that we could collect was limited by the limited capacities of individuals with long COVID. Furthermore, 32.5% of control participants were lost to follow-up because of COVID-19 infection, and some participants with long COVID were unable to continue follow-ups because of worsening symptoms. Notably, the higher representation of women compared to men in this study should be acknowledged as a limitation, as it may impact the generalizability of the findings. Finally, although GEE adjusted for baseline imbalances, residual confounding could not be entirely excluded, representing a limitation of this study. Despite these limitations, one of the strengths of our study is its comprehensive approach, integrating both objective measures of physical capacities and detailed evaluations of self-reported experiences, including fatigue, sleep quality, and pain. This holistic assessment over 3 follow-ups within 6 months offers a nuanced understanding of the impact of long COVID on overall well-being. Moreover, the inclusion of a CG enhances the robustness of our comparisons and clarity in interpreting the effects of long COVID. Additionally, our predictive analysis of HRQoL on the basis of initial assessments helps identify key indicators forecasting changes in quality of life over time, specifically highlighting baseline fatigue severity (FSS score) and daily step count as significant predictors. These findings underline the importance of early interventions targeting fatigue management and physical activity to improve long-term outcomes in individuals with long COVID.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: Our study confirms the significant impact of long COVID on both the physical capacity and quality of life. Although self-reported assessments of fatigue, sleep quality, and pain showed gradual improvement over a 6-month period, objective measures of physical capacity (such as the 6MWT and SPPB) improved—but not to a clinically significant extent. The physical capacities of participants with long COVID remained substantially lower compared to other groups. Moreover, predicting HRQoL at 6 months was negatively associated with fatigue but positively correlated with mean daily step count. These findings emphasize the importance of tailored interventions to help individuals manage fatigue and engage in safe, gradual physical activity to support recovery, and enhance quality of life.

sec.title: CRediT—CONTRIBUTOR ROLES
sec.p: Imane Salmam (Conceptualization [supporting], Data curation [lead], Formal analysis [lead], Investigation [lead], Methodology [lead], Project administration [equal], Resources [lead], Writing—original draft [lead], Writing—review & editing [equal]), François Desmeules (Conceptualization [lead], Data curation [supporting], Funding acquisition [equal], Investigation [equal], Project administration [equal], Resources [equal], Supervision [supporting], Validation [supporting], Visualization [supporting], Writing—review & editing [equal]), Kadija Perreault (Conceptualization [equal], Data curation [supporting], Funding acquisition [equal], Methodology [equal], Project administration [supporting], Supervision [equal], Validation [equal], Writing—review & editing [supporting]), Imane Zahouani (Conceptualization [equal], Data curation [equal], Funding acquisition [supporting], Methodology [supporting], Writing—review & editing [equal]), Simon Beaulieu-Bonneau (Conceptualization [equal], Formal analysis [equal], Funding acquisition [equal], Methodology [equal], Project administration [equal], Supervision [supporting], Validation [supporting], Writing—review & editing [equal]), Alexandre Campeau-Lecours (Data curation [equal], Formal analysis [supporting], Methodology [equal], Project administration [equal], Validation [supporting], Writing—review & Tittley (Conceptualization [equal], Data curation [supporting], Methodology [supporting], Software [lead], Writing—review & editing [supporting]), Gilles Drouin (Conceptualization [equal], Data curation [equal], Funding acquisition [equal], Methodology [supporting], Writing—review & editing [supporting]), Krista Best (Conceptualization [equal], Data curation [supporting], Funding acquisition [equal], Investigation [equal], Methodology [equal], Project administration [equal], Resources [equal], Supervision [supporting], Validation [supporting], Writing—review & editing [equal]), and Jean-Sébastien Roy (Conceptualization [lead], Data curation [supporting], Formal analysis [equal], Funding acquisition [lead], Investigation [supporting], Methodology [lead], Project administration [lead], Resources [equal], Software [equal], Supervision [lead], Validation [lead], Visualization [equal], Writing—original draft [supporting], Writing—review & editing [supporting]).

sec.title: FUNDING
sec.p: This research was funded by the Réseau Provincial de Recherche en Adaptation-Réadaptation (REPAR) and the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (CIHR; Emerging COVID-19 Research Gaps & Priorities—Post COVID-19 condition; Grant number: #466876), which had no role in the study design, data collection, analysis, or interpretation of the findings.

sec.title: ETHICS APPROVAL
sec.p: The study was approved by the Comité d'éthique de la recherche du CIUSSS de la Capitale-Nationale (2022-2328) and the Comité d'éthique de la recherche du CIUSSS de l'Est-de-l'Île-de-Montréal (2022-2811). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to their inclusion in the study.

sec.title: CLINICAL TRIAL REGISTRATION
sec.p: The study protocol was registered on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05216536).

sec.title: DISCLOSURES
sec.p: The authors completed the ICMJE Form for Disclosure of Potential Conflicts of Interest and reported no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY
sec.p: The data supporting the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
